1	---
2	name: lean-auditor
3	description: Whole-project read-only audit of all .lean code, with no strategy bias. Per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, bad Lean practices. Critical pushback on excuse-comments ("temporary wrong def", "will fix later") — treats them as red flags, not workflow.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [review]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - I am highly recommended every review phase. The audit warning
11	    fires if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.
12	
13	    **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
14	      - no `.lean` file under the project tree was modified this iter
15	        (the prover phase committed no edits — check via
16	        `git diff --stat HEAD~1 -- '*.lean'`);
17	      - my prior verdict had no must-fix-this-iter findings.
18	
19	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`.
20	  - Pass me as LITTLE strategic context as possible. Do NOT include
21	    STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, or "this is what we are trying to prove"
22	    framing in the directive. My value depends on auditing the Lean
23	    *without* bias toward what the strategy claims should be true.
24	  - Acceptable directive content: a list of files, optional focus areas
25	    (e.g. "pay extra attention to Algebra/"), and the absolute paths
26	    to read. That's it.
27	  - My output is a per-file checklist plus a flagged-issues block. Use
28	    it to seed `recommendations.md` and (when severe) trigger a
29	    structural-refactor directive (via the catalog's refactor
30	    subagent) next iter.
31	  - I audit the Lean as Lean. The catalog may include a separate
32	    Lean ↔ blueprint checker that compares Lean against the
33	    chapter — run both for full coverage when both are present.
34	---
35	
36	# Lean Auditor
37	
38	You read **every `.lean` file in the project** and produce a per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, and bad Lean practices. You are **read-only** and your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
39	
40	You are **highly recommended in the review phase**: the review agent dispatches you whenever any `.lean` file has been modified this iter, OR whenever the prior verdict had live must-fix findings. Your output flags issues the prover and plan agents may have missed.
41	
42	## Stance: no strategic bias
43	
44	You audit the Lean **as Lean**. Your directive will NOT include the project strategy, the blueprint, or "what we are trying to prove" — and that is intentional. The point is to detect mathematically-wrong definitions and dead-end proofs *without* being swayed by what the project assumes should be true. A definition is wrong if it is wrong by reading the Lean alone (e.g. its body contradicts standard mathematical usage of the named concept); you do not need to know the strategy to spot that.
45	
46	This stance is also what makes you **critical of excuse-comments**. Lean-side comments like:
47	
48	- `-- We decided to use a temporary wrong definition; will fix it later.`
49	- `-- This proof is incomplete but works for now.`
50	- `-- TODO: replace before merging.`
51	- `-- Stand-in definition until we figure out the right one.`
52	
53	are RED FLAGS, not project workflow. Authors writing such comments are admitting the code is wrong; the comments cannot be allowed to silence the alarm. Flag every such comment with severity **major** at minimum — and **critical** when the named declaration is load-bearing.
54	
55	## Directive Format
56	
57	```markdown
58	# Lean Auditor Directive
59	
60	## Slug
61	<slug>
62	
63	## Scope (files)
64	<"all" by default. Optionally a list of file paths if the review agent wants to narrow scope; never expand beyond the project.>
65	
66	## Focus areas (optional)
67	<directories or namespaces to pay extra attention to. Bias toward thoroughness — do not skip the rest of the project just because focus is named.>
68	
69	## Known issues
70	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
71	```
72	
73	The directive **does not** include STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, references, or a description of what the project is trying to prove. That is by design.
74	
75	## What you do
76	
77	1. Read your directive.
78	2. **List every `.lean` file** under the project. Use `find` or `ls -R` — do not rely on import graphs (some files may be temporarily orphaned).
79	3. **For each file** (every one, no scope shortcuts):
80	   - Read the whole file.
81	   - For each declaration: is the signature reasonable as Lean? Is the body suspect (uses `axiom`, `Classical.choice _`, `sorry` with a body that looks like fake content, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim)?
82	   - For each comment: is it stale (referencing removed code or old strategies)? Is it an *excuse-comment* (see above)? Is it accurate about the code below it?
83	   - For each proof: is the tactic chain reasonable for the goal type, or does it look like it is going in the wrong direction (e.g. unfolding to manipulate syntax instead of using the available structure lemmas)?
84	   - Note any bad Lean practices: misuse of universe polymorphism, redundant typeclasses, deprecated tactics, anti-patterns like proving `False`-style lemmas to bypass infrastructure.
85	4. **Produce a per-file checklist** + a flagged-issues block grouped by severity.
86	
87	You may use:
88	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics. Useful to verify a declaration's actual type.
89	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to get sorry counts per file.
90	- Standard `Read` / `Grep` for code reading.
91	
92	You may NOT modify any project file. Even when a fix is obvious.
93	
94	## Report format
95	
96	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-auditor-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
97	
98	```markdown
99	# Lean Audit Report
100	
101	## Slug
102	<slug>
103	
104	## Iteration
105	<NNN>
106	
107	## Scope
108	- files audited: <N>
109	- files skipped (per directive): <N> — <reason>
110	
111	## Per-file checklist
112	
113	### <path/to/file.lean>
114	- **outdated comments**: <N flagged> (or "none")
115	- **suspect definitions**: <N flagged> (or "none")
116	- **dead-end proofs**: <N flagged> (or "none")
117	- **bad practices**: <N flagged> (or "none")
118	- **excuse-comments**: <N flagged> (or "none")
119	- **notes**:
120	  - <one bullet per finding, naming line number where relevant>
121	  - ...
122	
123	### <next file>
124	- ...
125	
126	(One block per file. Cover every file, including the ones with no findings — they get `none` everywhere and `notes: -`.)
127	
128	## Must-fix-this-iter
129	
130	Apply verbatim. Every one of the following lands here automatically, no exceptions, no under-classification:
131	
132	- **Excuse-comments** on any declaration: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- placeholder`, `-- temporary`, `-- wrong but works`, `-- will fix later`. These are admissions that the code is wrong; treat them as wrong.
133	- **Weakened-wrong definitions**: a `def Foo := <something structurally different>` standing in for the real concept (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic …` when the real definition is an invertible quasi-coherent sheaf).
134	- **Parallel APIs of existing Mathlib**: a project file that copy-and-modifies a Mathlib definition (changing one type-class or argument) instead of instancing the existing Mathlib version. Bridge lemmas needed to consume it are the symptom.
135	- **Suspect bodies on substantive claims**: `:= sorry` on a load-bearing claim, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial statement, `:= Classical.choice _` without authorization.
136	- **Axioms** on non-trivial claims that the project hasn't explicitly authorized in the strategy.
137	
138	A finding that meets any of the above lands at must-fix-this-iter — even if classifying it as "major" would feel more diplomatic. Soft severity is how wrong code hardens into the project.
139	
140	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>. Why must-fix: <one line>.
141	- ...
142	
143	## Major
144	
145	Findings that are real issues but don't meet the must-fix bar — typically stale comments that don't actively mislead, naming drift, minor code smells.
146	
147	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>.
148	- ...
149	
150	## Minor
151	
152	Low-impact observations.
153	
154	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>.
155	- ...
156	
157	## Excuse-comments (always called out separately)
158	
159	If any excuse-comments were flagged, list each one verbatim with its file:line. These deserve special visibility because they document the project lying to itself.
160	
161	- `Foo/Bar.lean:42`: "TODO: replace with the real definition" (attached to `Foo.bar`, which is a load-bearing definition in `Baz.lean`). Severity: critical.
162	- ...
163	
164	## Severity summary
165	
166	- **must-fix-this-iter**: <N> — these block downstream work in their files until addressed (see plan.md's per-file gate).
167	- **major**: <N>
168	- **minor**: <N>
169	- **excuse-comments**: <N> (also counted under must-fix-this-iter above; called out separately because they document the project lying to itself).
170	
171	Overall verdict: one sentence.
172	```
173	
174	**Strict severity reminder.** The plan agent's gate treats must-fix-this-iter as blocking. If you under-classify a wrong-decision finding to keep the loop's momentum, you are working against the project — wrong code accumulates faster than reviewers can catch it later. When in doubt, classify higher, not lower.
175	
176	## Return value
177	
178	Your final assistant message:
179	
180	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> files audited, <M> issues (critical/major/minor: a/b/c)`
181	- The path to your full report.
182	
183	## Reminders
184	
185	- **Read every file. No scope shortcuts.**
186	- **No strategic context.** Your audit is unbiased by design.
187	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
188	- **Excuse-comments are red flags.** Critical or major severity, every time.
189	- **Per-file checklist is the primary output.** Cover every file; the format is fixed.
190

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

6	import Mathlib
7	import AlgebraicJacobian.Cohomology.HigherDirectImage
8	
9	/-!
10	# Čech computation of the higher direct images `Rⁱ f_*` (unconditional)
11	
12	This file constructs the higher derived direct images `Rⁱ f_* F` for `i ≥ 1`
13	**without appealing to injective resolutions** in the category of sheaves of
14	modules. The companion `Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean` defines `Rⁱ f_*` as a
15	right derived functor, which requires the ambient category of `O_X`-modules to
16	have enough injectives — a property not currently available for sheaves of
17	modules over a sheaf of rings whose value category varies over the site. The
18	Čech approach developed here sidesteps the issue: it computes `Rⁱ f_* F` as the
19	cohomology of an explicit complex built from the pushforwards of `F` over the
20	finite intersections of an affine open cover, producing an **unconditional**
21	construction of `Rⁱ f_*` for quasi-coherent `F` and separated quasi-compact `f`.
22	
23	Throughout, `f : X ⟶ S` is a quasi-compact, separated morphism of schemes (so
24	all finite intersections of an affine open cover of `X` are again affine), and
25	`F : X.Modules` is a quasi-coherent `O_X`-module. A base change of `f` along
26	`g : S' ⟶ S` is recorded by a cartesian square
27	```
28	  X' --g'--> X
29	  |f'        |f
30	  v          v
31	  S' --g---> S
32	```
33	with `F' = (g')^* F` the pullback of `F` to `X'`.
34	
35	The six main declarations are:
36	
37	* `AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve` — the (augmented) Čech nerve of an affine open
38	  cover, an augmented cosimplicial object of `O_X`-modules.
39	* `AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex` — the relative Čech complex in `QCoh(S)`, a
40	  cochain complex of `O_S`-modules whose degree-`p` term is the product of the
41	  pushforwards of `F` over the `(p+1)`-fold intersections of the cover.
42	* `AlgebraicGeometry.CechAcyclic.affine` — Čech acyclicity on affines: the Čech
43	  complex of a standard cover of an affine scheme has vanishing cohomology in
44	  all positive degrees (Serre vanishing for quasi-coherent sheaves on affines).
45	* `AlgebraicGeometry.cech_computes_higherDirectImage` — the cohomology of the
46	  relative Čech complex is canonically isomorphic to `Rⁱ f_* F` wherever the
47	  derived functor is defined.
48	* `AlgebraicGeometry.cechHigherDirectImage` — the **unconditional** `i`-th higher
49	  direct image, defined as the `i`-th cohomology sheaf of the relative Čech
50	  complex (no enough-injectives hypothesis required).
51	* `AlgebraicGeometry.cech_flatBaseChange` — flat base change for the
52	  unconditional Čech higher direct images.
53	
54	See `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage.tex`.
55	
56	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, §Čech cohomology of quasi-coherent
57	sheaves and §Quasi-coherence of higher direct images; Tags 02KE
58	(`lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent`), 02KG
59	(`lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero`), 02KH
60	(`lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology`).
61	-/
62	
63	universe u
64	
65	open CategoryTheory Limits
66	
67	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
68	
69	open Scheme.Modules
70	
71	variable {S S' X X' : Scheme.{u}}
72	
73	/-- **Čech nerve of an affine open cover** (Stacks, Cohomology of Schemes, §Čech
74	cohomology of quasi-coherent sheaves).
75	
76	For a scheme `X`, a finite affine open cover `𝒰 : X = ⋃ Uᵢ` and a quasi-coherent
77	sheaf `F`, the *Čech nerve* is the augmented cosimplicial object of
78	`O_X`-modules whose object in simplicial degree `p` is the product, over the
79	`(p+1)`-tuples `(i₀,…,i_p)` of indices, of the direct images
80	`(j_{i₀…i_p})_* (F|_{U_{i₀…i_p}})` of the restriction of `F` to the
81	`(p+1)`-fold intersection `U_{i₀…i_p} = U_{i₀} ∩ ⋯ ∩ U_{i_p}` along the open
82	immersion `j_{i₀…i_p} : U_{i₀…i_p} ↪ X`. Faces are the restriction maps that
83	omit one index, degeneracies repeat one index, and the augmentation in degree
84	`-1` is `F` itself on all of `X`. When `X` is separated each intersection
85	`U_{i₀…i_p}` is affine.
86	
87	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
88	`lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial`. -/
89	noncomputable def CechNerve (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) (F : X.Modules) :
90	    CosimplicialObject.Augmented X.Modules :=
91	  -- Construction (Stacks): the augmented cosimplicial object
92	  -- `[p] ↦ ∏_{(i₀,…,i_p)} (j_{i₀…i_p})_* (F|_{U_{i₀…i_p}})` with faces the
93	  -- index-omitting restriction maps, degeneracies the index-repeating maps, and
94	  -- augmentation `F`. Requires the nerve of the cover (intersection products of
95	  -- pushforwards) as a functor out of `SimplexCategory`, currently absent from
96	  -- Mathlib for `Scheme.Modules`.
97	  sorry
98	
99	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — scheme-level Čech nerve backbone
100	
101	The genuine construction of the {\v C}ech nerve `CechNerve` factors through two
102	ingredients that are independent of one another:
103	
104	* a *geometric* backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of the cover, an
105	  augmented simplicial **scheme** over `X` (the iterated fibre powers of
106	  `∐ᵢ Uᵢ` over `X`), which exists unconditionally because `Scheme` has all
107	  finite limits; and
108	* a *push-pull* functor `(Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules`, `(Y, p) ↦ p_* p^* F`, that
109	  turns the simplicial scheme over `X` into the cosimplicial `O_X`-module
110	  `CechNerve`.
111	
112	The backbone (`coverArrow`, `coverCechNerve`) is built here axiom-clean. The
113	push-pull functor is the remaining gap: its `map_comp` requires the
114	`pushforwardComp` / `pullbackComp` coherence isomorphisms (the same coherence
115	quagmire active in `Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean`), so `CechNerve` itself is
116	left as the single genuine hole.
117	
118	Independently of the nerve, the passage *from* an augmented cosimplicial
119	`O_X`-module *to* the relative {\v C}ech cochain complex in `QCoh(S)` is pure,
120	coherence-free plumbing (`relativeCechComplexOfNerve`): forget the augmentation,
121	push forward along `f` via `CosimplicialObject.whiskering`, and take the
122	alternating coface-map cochain complex. We record it here so that `CechComplex`
123	is *defined* in terms of `CechNerve` — closing `CechNerve` axiom-clean
124	immediately yields an axiom-clean `CechComplex`. -/
125	
126	/-- The arrow `∐ᵢ Uᵢ ⟶ X` (`Sigma.desc 𝒰.f`) attached to an open cover `𝒰` of a
127	scheme `X`. Its augmented {\v C}ech nerve is the geometric backbone of the
128	relative {\v C}ech complex. Project-local: packages the cover as a single arrow
129	so the existing `Arrow.augmentedCechNerve` machinery applies. -/
130	noncomputable def coverArrow (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) : Arrow Scheme.{u} :=
131	  Arrow.mk (Sigma.desc 𝒰.f)
132	
133	/-- The augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an open cover `𝒰`, as an augmented
134	simplicial scheme over `X`: in simplicial degree `p` it is the `(p+1)`-fold
135	fibre power of `∐ᵢ Uᵢ` over `X`, i.e. `∐_{(i₀,…,i_p)} U_{i₀} ×ₓ ⋯ ×ₓ U_{i_p}`,
136	with augmentation the cover map to `X`. Exists unconditionally because `Scheme`
137	has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks used by
138	`Arrow.augmentedCechNerve`). Project-local geometric backbone for `CechNerve`. -/
139	noncomputable def coverCechNerve (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) :
140	    SimplicialObject.Augmented Scheme.{u} :=
141	  (coverArrow 𝒰).augmentedCechNerve
142	
143	/-! ### Push–pull functor `G : (Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules` — object and morphism bricks
144	
145	The geometric backbone above is lifted to a cosimplicial `O_X`-module by the
146	*relative-direct-image functor on the over-category*
147	```
148	  G : (Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules,   (Y, p) ↦ p_* p^* F,
149	```
150	sending an `X`-scheme `p : Y ⟶ X` to the pushforward along `p` of the pullback
151	`p^* F`. We record here the two *pre-coherence* bricks of `G` — its action on
152	objects (`pushPullObj`, the planner's `Gobj`) and on morphisms (`pushPullMap`,
153	the planner's `Gmap`) — both axiom-clean and free of any functor law. The functor
154	laws `G(𝟙) = 𝟙` and `G(g ≫ h) = G(h) ≫ G(g)` are a *consumer* of the
155	pushforward/pullback composition coherence (`pushforwardComp` / `pullbackComp` and
156	their unitor/pentagon identities) and are deferred; see the note after
157	`pushPullMap`. -/
158	
159	/-- The object map of the push–pull functor `G : (Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules`,
160	`(Y, p) ↦ p_* p^* F`. Sends an `X`-scheme `Y` (with structure map `Y.hom : Y.left
161	⟶ X`) to the pushforward along `Y.hom` of the pullback of `F`. Project-local
162	object brick of the {\v C}ech push–pull functor (the planner's `Gobj`). -/
163	noncomputable def pushPullObj (F : X.Modules) (Y : Over X) : X.Modules :=
164	  (pushforward Y.hom).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)
165	
166	/-- The morphism map of the push–pull functor `G : (Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules`. For a
167	morphism `g : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁` of `X`-schemes (so `g.left ≫ Y₁.hom = Y₂.hom`, the
168	over-triangle `Over.w g`), the contravariant functor produces the restriction
169	comparison `pushPullObj F Y₁ ⟶ pushPullObj F Y₂`. It is the three-step composite:
170	the unit `η` of the adjunction `pullback g.left ⊣ pushforward g.left`, the
171	pushforward comparison `(pushforwardComp g.left Y₁.hom).hom`, and the pushforward
172	of the pullback comparison `(pullbackComp g.left Y₁.hom).hom`, glued by two
173	`eqToHom` transports along the over-triangle `Over.w g`. No functor law is used:
174	this is a reusable pre-coherence brick (the planner's `Gmap`). -/
175	noncomputable def pushPullMap (F : X.Modules) {Y₁ Y₂ : Over X} (g : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁) :
176	    pushPullObj F Y₁ ⟶ pushPullObj F Y₂ :=
177	  (pushforward Y₁.hom).map
178	      ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g.left).unit.app
179	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y₁.hom).obj F)) ≫
180	    (pushforwardComp g.left Y₁.hom).hom.app
181	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g.left).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y₁.hom).obj F)) ≫
182	    eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (pushforward q).obj
183	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g.left).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y₁.hom).obj F)))
184	      (Over.w g)) ≫
185	    (pushforward Y₂.hom).map ((pullbackComp g.left Y₁.hom).hom.app F) ≫
186	    eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (pushforward Y₂.hom).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F))
187	      (Over.w g))
188	
189	/- **The functor laws `pushPullMap_id` / `pushPullMap_comp`.**
190	Assembling `pushPullObj` / `pushPullMap` into the functor `G : (Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤
191	X.Modules` requires
192	```
193	  pushPullMap_id   : pushPullMap F (𝟙 Y) = 𝟙 (pushPullObj F Y)          -- DONE (below)
194	  pushPullMap_comp : pushPullMap F (g ≫ h) = pushPullMap F h ≫ pushPullMap F g  -- remaining
195	```
196	Both are pure pushforward/pullback *coherence* statements with no sectionwise
197	content of their own. The route is the adjunction-mate calculus: the
198	`unit ≫ pushforwardComp.hom` head of `pushPullMap` is governed by `unit_conjugateEquiv`
199	and `conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv` / `conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom`, and the `eqToHom`
200	transports are discharged against the pseudofunctor unitor/pentagon identities
201	(`pseudofunctor_right_unitality` for `id`, `pseudofunctor_associativity` for `comp`, all in
202	`Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Modules/Sheaf.lean`).
203	
204	`pushPullMap_id` is closed axiom-clean below. A plain `simp` (even with the coherence lemmas)
205	makes no progress on the head `unit ≫ pushforwardComp.hom`, and the sectionwise `ext` route
206	exposes the pullback adjunction unit (not sectionwise trivial); the working proof instead routes
207	through `unit_conjugateEquiv` of the identity adjunction (`star`), the right-unitality of the
208	pullback comparison (`hru`), and a `Scheme.Modules.hom_ext; intro U; rfl` collapse of the
209	sectionwise-trivial pushforward coercion (`hpf`). `pushPullMap_comp` follows the same template
210	with `pseudofunctor_associativity` (the pentagon) in place of right-unitality — see the comment
211	above its (currently unfilled) statement. -/
212	
213	/-! ### Functor laws of the push–pull functor `G` -/
214	
215	/-- Identity law of the push–pull functor `G`. -/
216	lemma pushPullMap_id (F : X.Modules) (Y : Over X) :
217	    pushPullMap F (𝟙 Y) = 𝟙 (pushPullObj F Y) := by
218	  -- `star`: the unit-triangle for the identity adjunction `pullback 𝟙 ⊣ pushforward 𝟙`.
219	  have star := unit_conjugateEquiv (Adjunction.id (C := Y.left.Modules))
220	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (𝟙 Y.left))
221	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackId Y.left).hom ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)
222	  rw [Scheme.Modules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom] at star
223	  simp only [Adjunction.id_unit, NatTrans.id_app, Functor.id_obj] at star
224	  -- `hru`: right-unitality of the pullback pseudofunctor, applied at `F`.
225	  have hru := Scheme.Modules.pseudofunctor_right_unitality (X := Y.left) (f := Y.hom)
226	  have hru2 := congrArg (fun t => NatTrans.app t F) hru
227	  simp only [NatTrans.comp_app, Functor.whiskerLeft_app, Functor.rightUnitor_hom_app] at hru2
228	  have hpf : (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp (𝟙 Y.left) Y.hom).hom.app
229	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (𝟙 Y.left)).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)) ≫
230	      eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward q).obj
231	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (𝟙 Y.left)).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)))
232	        (Category.id_comp Y.hom)) =
233	      (Scheme.Modules.pushforward Y.hom).map ((Scheme.Modules.pushforwardId Y.left).hom.app
234	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (𝟙 Y.left)).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F))) := by
235	    apply Scheme.Modules.hom_ext
236	    intro U; rfl
237	  -- the unit zig-zag for the identity adjunction collapses on `M`
238	  have hzig : (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (𝟙 Y.left)).unit.app
239	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F) ≫
240	      (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardId Y.left).hom.app
241	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (𝟙 Y.left)).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)) ≫
242	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackId Y.left).hom.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F) =
243	      𝟙 ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F) := by
244	    have hnat := (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardId Y.left).hom.naturality
245	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackId Y.left).hom.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F))
246	    simp only [Functor.id_map] at hnat
247	    erw [← hnat, ← reassoc_of% star]
248	    exact Iso.inv_hom_id_app _ _
249	  -- the pullback comparison + the over-triangle transport collapse via right-unitality
250	  have hib_inner : (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (𝟙 Y.left) Y.hom).hom.app F ≫
251	      eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F) (Category.id_comp Y.hom)) =
252	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackId Y.left).hom.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F) := by
253	    rw [eqToHom_app] at hru2
254	    rw [← hru2, ← Category.assoc, Iso.hom_inv_id_app]; simp
255	  have hib : (Scheme.Modules.pushforward Y.hom).map
256	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (𝟙 Y.left) Y.hom).hom.app F) ≫
257	      eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward Y.hom).obj
258	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)) (Category.id_comp Y.hom)) =
259	      (Scheme.Modules.pushforward Y.hom).map
260	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackId Y.left).hom.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)) := by
261	    have he : eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward Y.hom).obj
262	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)) (Category.id_comp Y.hom)) =
263	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward Y.hom).map
264	          (eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)
265	            (Category.id_comp Y.hom))) := by
266	      rw [eqToHom_map]
267	    rw [he, ← Functor.map_comp]; exact congrArg _ hib_inner
268	  -- assemble
269	  simp only [pushPullMap, Over.id_left]
270	  erw [reassoc_of% hpf, hib, ← Functor.map_comp]
271	  erw [hzig, CategoryTheory.Functor.map_id]; rfl
272	
273	/- **Composition law of the push–pull functor `G` (contravariant) — remaining.**
274	The identity law `pushPullMap_id` above is closed axiom-clean by the mate calculus
275	(`unit_conjugateEquiv` + `conjugateEquiv_pullbackId_hom` + `pseudofunctor_right_unitality`).
276	The composition law
277	```
278	  pushPullMap_comp : pushPullMap F (g ≫ h) = pushPullMap F h ≫ pushPullMap F g
279	```
280	follows the *same* template one step up — but with the pentagon
281	`Scheme.Modules.pseudofunctor_associativity (f := g.left) (g := h.left) (h := Y₁.hom)`
282	(verified to typecheck) in place of `pseudofunctor_right_unitality`, the composite-adjunction
283	unit expansion `Adjunction.comp_unit_app` for `η_{g.left ≫ h.left}`, and
284	`Adjunction.unit_naturality` to slide the inner unit past the outer pushforward. The pushforward
285	coercions (`pushforwardComp ≫ eqToHom`) collapse sectionwise by `Scheme.Modules.hom_ext; intro U; rfl`
286	exactly as the `hpf` helper of `pushPullMap_id`. It is a longer (~150-LOC) pentagon calculation and is
287	left for a focused follow-up pass; assembling `pushPullFunctor` needs it together with `pushPullMap_id`.
288	
289	The mate core that splits the composite unit is isolated as the reusable lemma
290	`pushPull_unit_mate` below (axiom-clean): it rewrites the head
291	`p_*(η^f) ≫ pushforwardComp` as `η^{f≫p} ≫ (f≫p)_*(pullbackComp⁻¹)`.
292	
293	**iter-271 breakthrough — the kernel `whnf` wall is now BYPASSED.** The earlier
294	blocker was a kernel `whnf` blow-up when cancelling `pushPullMap`'s two over-triangle
295	`eqToHom` transports *in situ* (at the concrete `pushforward`/`pullback` comparison
296	objects). The reusable brick `pushPull_transport_cancel` below resolves it: it states
297	the cancellation with the over-triangle equality `h : gl ≫ p₁ = p₂` as a **free
298	hypothesis**, proven by a single `subst h` on *abstract* objects (transports become
299	`eqToHom rfl = 𝟙`, kernel-cheap), and is applied to `pushPullMap` via `erw` — which
300	rewrites the tail without forcing the kernel to unfold the comparison objects. (Plain
301	`rw` does not fire: `SheafOfModules` comps are defeq-not-syntactic, so `erw` is
302	mandatory.) Applying `erw [pushPull_transport_cancel …]` to all three `pushPullMap`
303	occurrences (LHS once, RHS twice) was verified to fire with NO kernel blow-up, leaving
304	both sides transport-light. `pushforwardComp` is `Iso.refl` (strictly functorial), so
305	its comparison factor is `𝟙` and is absorbed by the same `erw`.
306	
307	**Remaining work for `pushPullMap_comp` — a clean post-`subst` pentagon (no kernel
308	issues).** After the three `erw`s, `subst`-ing the two free over-triangles (via a
309	generalised auxiliary lemma with `p₂, p₃` and the over-triangles as free hypotheses,
310	mirroring `pushPull_transport_cancel`) clears the remaining transports except the
311	single associativity cell `eqToHom ((kl≫gl₂)≫p₁ = kl≫(gl₂≫p₁))`. The residual goal is
312	the pure pseudofunctor pentagon: it closes by `pushPull_unit_mate kl gl₂` (to convert
313	the composite unit `η^{kl≫gl₂}`) together with
314	`Scheme.Modules.pseudofunctor_associativity (f := kl) (g := gl₂) (h := p₁)` — whose four
315	`pullbackComp` 2-cells (`pullbackComp kl (gl₂≫p₁)`, `pullbackComp gl₂ p₁`,
316	`pullbackComp kl gl₂`, `pullbackComp (kl≫gl₂) p₁`) match exactly the four appearing in
317	the goal. The decompositions `pushforward (a≫b) = pushforward a ⋙ pushforward b` and
318	`(pushforward (a≫b)).map φ = (pushforward b).map ((pushforward a).map φ)` both hold by
319	`rfl`. The only friction left is the whiskered-pentagon + `eqToHom` bookkeeping (the
320	standard defeq-not-syntactic `erw` grind), a ~60-100 LOC follow-up; the kernel obstacle
321	that blocked five prior iterations is gone. -/
322	
323	/-- **Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push–pull head.**
324	For composable scheme morphisms `f : A ⟶ B`, `p : B ⟶ Z` and `N : Z.Modules`, the
325	adjunction unit at `N` for `p` followed by the *head* of `pushPullMap` (the
326	pushforward of the unit for `f`, then the pushforward comparison) equals the unit
327	for the composite `f ≫ p` followed by the pushforward of the inverse pullback
328	comparison. This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
329	`η^{f≫p}` into the iterated units `η^p`, `η^f`; it is the reusable ingredient that
330	the functoriality (pentagon) law of `pushPullMap` repeatedly consumes when
331	splitting a composite unit. Project-local supplement. -/
332	lemma pushPull_unit_mate {A B Z : Scheme.{u}} (f : A ⟶ B) (p : B ⟶ Z)
333	    (N : Z.Modules) :
334	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction p).unit.app N ≫
335	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p).map
336	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f).unit.app
337	            ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p).obj N)) ≫
338	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp f p).hom.app
339	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback f).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p).obj N)) =
340	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (f ≫ p)).unit.app N ≫
341	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward (f ≫ p)).map
342	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp f p).inv.app N) := by
343	  have key := unit_conjugateEquiv
344	    ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction p).comp
345	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f))
346	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (f ≫ p))
347	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp f p).inv N
348	  rw [Scheme.Modules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv, Adjunction.comp_unit_app] at key
349	  simpa only [Category.assoc] using key
350	
351	/-- **Over-triangle transport cancellation for the push–pull tail** (kernel-cheap
352	generalised form). The morphism map `pushPullMap` glues its pullback-comparison leg
353	to the target object `pushPullObj F Y₂` by two `eqToHom` coercions along the
354	over-triangle `g.left ≫ Y₁.hom = Y₂.hom`. Cancelling those coercions *in situ*
355	(at the concrete pushforward/pullback objects) provokes a kernel `whnf` blow-up.
356	This lemma states the cancellation **with the over-triangle equality as a free
357	hypothesis** `h : gl ≫ p₁ = p₂`, so the proof is a single `subst h` (after which
358	the transports become `eqToHom rfl = 𝟙` and vanish — kernel-cheap) followed by
359	`simp`. Applying it to `pushPullMap` via `rw` rewrites the tail without forcing the
360	kernel to unfold the comparison objects: the over-triangle leg
361	`eqToHom ≫ (pushforward p₂).map (pullbackComp).hom ≫ eqToHom` collapses to the
362	transport-light `(pushforward (gl ≫ p₁)).map (pullbackComp).hom ≫ eqToHom`, the
363	single residual `eqToHom` carrying the unavoidable object identification of the
364	codomain `pushPullObj F Y₂`. Reusable pre-coherence brick for `pushPullMap_comp`. -/
365	lemma pushPull_transport_cancel {Y₁ Y₂ : Scheme.{u}}
366	    (gl : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁) (p₁ : Y₁ ⟶ X) (p₂ : Y₂ ⟶ X)
367	    (h : gl ≫ p₁ = p₂) (F : X.Modules) :
368	    eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward q).obj
369	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback gl).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p₁).obj F))) h) ≫
370	      (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p₂).map ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp gl p₁).hom.app F) ≫
371	      eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p₂).obj
372	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)) h) =
373	    (Scheme.Modules.pushforward (gl ≫ p₁)).map
374	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp gl p₁).hom.app F) ≫
375	      eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward q).obj
376	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)) h) := by
377	  subst h
378	  simp
379	
380	/-- **Composite-unit decomposition for the push–pull head.** The adjunction unit
381	`η^{f≫p}` for a composite morphism, expressed through the iterated units `η^p`,
382	`η^f` and the pushforward/pullback comparison isomorphisms. This is the
383	`pushPull_unit_mate` identity solved for `η^{f≫p}` (post-composing with
384	`(f≫p)_*(pullbackComp).hom` cancels the `pullbackComp.inv` factor). Reusable brick
385	for the composition law of `pushPullMap`. -/
386	lemma pushPull_unit_comp {A B Z : Scheme.{u}} (f : A ⟶ B) (p : B ⟶ Z)
387	    (N : Z.Modules) :
388	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (f ≫ p)).unit.app N =
389	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction p).unit.app N ≫
390	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p).map
391	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f).unit.app
392	            ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p).obj N)) ≫
393	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp f p).hom.app
394	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback f).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p).obj N)) ≫
395	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward (f ≫ p)).map
396	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp f p).hom.app N) := by
397	  have m := pushPull_unit_mate f p N
398	  erw [reassoc_of% m, ← Functor.map_comp, Iso.inv_hom_id_app,
399	    CategoryTheory.Functor.map_id, Category.comp_id]
400	
401	/-- The pushforward pseudofunctor is *strict* on sheaves of modules: the
402	`pushforwardComp` comparison `2`-cell is the identity on the nose. Holds by `rfl`
403	(`pushforward (a ≫ p) = pushforward a ⋙ pushforward p` definitionally). Project-local
404	collapse used to discharge the pushforward legs of the push–pull pentagon. -/
405	lemma pushforwardComp_hom_app_id {Z₁ Z₂ Z₃ : Scheme.{u}} (a : Z₂ ⟶ Z₁) (p : Z₁ ⟶ Z₃)
406	    (M : Z₂.Modules) : (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp a p).hom.app M = 𝟙 _ :=
407	  rfl

409	/-- **Scheme-level push–pull comparison map with the over-triangle as a free
410	hypothesis.** This is the body of `pushPullMap` with the underlying scheme map
411	`a`, the two structure maps `p₁ p₂`, and the over-triangle `w : a ≫ p₁ = p₂`
412	generalised away from the `Over X` packaging. Spelling the over-triangle as a free
413	hypothesis is what makes the composition law provable by `subst` (after which the
414	`eqToHom` over-triangle transports become `eqToHom rfl = 𝟙` and vanish, dodging the
415	kernel `whnf` wall). By construction `pushPullMap F g = rawPushPullMap g.left Y₁.hom
416	Y₂.hom (Over.w g) F` definitionally. Project-local. -/
417	noncomputable def rawPushPullMap {Z₁ Z₂ : Scheme.{u}} (a : Z₂ ⟶ Z₁)
418	    (p₁ : Z₁ ⟶ X) (p₂ : Z₂ ⟶ X) (w : a ≫ p₁ = p₂) (F : X.Modules) :
419	    (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p₁).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p₁).obj F) ⟶
420	      (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p₂).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p₂).obj F) :=
421	  (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p₁).map
422	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction a).unit.app
423	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p₁).obj F)) ≫
424	    (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp a p₁).hom.app
425	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback a).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p₁).obj F)) ≫
426	    eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward q).obj
427	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback a).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p₁).obj F))) w) ≫
428	    (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p₂).map ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp a p₁).hom.app F) ≫
429	    eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p₂).obj
430	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)) w)

433	/-- `pushPullMap` is the `Over X`-instance of `rawPushPullMap`. Holds by `rfl`. -/
434	lemma pushPullMap_eq_raw (F : X.Modules) {Y₁ Y₂ : Over X} (g : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁) :
435	    pushPullMap F g = rawPushPullMap g.left Y₁.hom Y₂.hom (Over.w g) F :=
436	  rfl

438	/- **Composition law `pushPullMap_comp` — reduced to an explicit clean pentagon, not
439	yet closed.** The kernel `whnf` wall that blocked iters 264/265/271 is fully removed:
440	the route is `pushPullMap F g = rawPushPullMap g.left Y₁.hom Y₂.hom (Over.w g) F`
441	(`pushPullMap_eq_raw`, by `rfl`), reducing `pushPullMap_comp` to
442	`rawPushPullMap_comp`, which has the two over-triangles `wg : a ≫ p₁ = p₂`,
443	`wh : b ≫ p₂ = p₃` as **free hypotheses**. `subst wg; subst wh` then makes every
444	`eqToHom` over-triangle transport `eqToHom rfl = 𝟙` and vanish (kernel-cheap — no
445	`whnf` blow-up). After `simp only [rawPushPullMap, eqToHom_refl]`,
446	`rw [pushPull_unit_comp b a _]` (the composite-unit decomposition),
447	`simp only [pushforwardComp_hom_app_id]` (pushforward is *strict*: the comparison
448	2-cells are `𝟙` by `rfl`) and absorbing the resulting defeq-`𝟙`s by
449	`repeat erw [Category.id_comp]`, the goal becomes the genuine **pullback
450	pseudofunctor pentagon** (no transports, no `pushforwardComp`):
451	
452	  `(pushforward p₁).map (a_*(η^b) ≫ (b≫a)_*(pullbackComp b a).hom.app(p₁^*F)) ≫`
453	  `  (pushforward (b≫a≫p₁)).map ((pullbackComp (b≫a) p₁).hom.app F) ≫ eqToHom`
454	  ` = (pushforward (a≫p₁)).map (η^b ≫ (pullback b ⋙ pushforward b).map((pullbackComp a p₁).hom.app F)) ≫`
455	  `  (pushforward (b≫a≫p₁)).map ((pullbackComp b (a≫p₁)).hom.app F)`
456	
457	where the leading `(pushforward p₁).map (η^a)` has already been peeled and the `η^b`
458	units aligned by `(pullbackPushforwardAdjunction b).unit.naturality`. The residual
459	content is `Scheme.Modules.pseudofunctor_associativity (f := b) (g := a) (h := p₁)`
460	(its four `pullbackComp` 2-cells `pullbackComp b a`, `pullbackComp (b≫a) p₁`,
461	`pullbackComp a p₁`, `pullbackComp b (a≫p₁)` match exactly) together with the single
462	associativity cell `eqToHom ((b≫a)≫p₁ = b≫(a≫p₁))`.
463	
464	**Why it is not yet closed (next-prover dead-ends, all hit this iter):** the regime is
465	pervasively *defeq-not-syntactic*. `rw`/`reassoc_of%` of `Category.assoc`,
466	`Functor.map_comp`, the lead `rfl`-bridge, and `pseudofunctor_associativity` all fail
467	to match visually-identical terms (the pushforward/`SheafOfModules` `≫` and `.map`
468	carry non-syntactic instances). `erw` matches but **whnf-unfolds `pullbackComp` into
469	its raw `TwoSquare.equivNatTrans`/`mateEquiv` mate definition**, exploding the goal.
470	`congr 1` on the defeq-but-not-syntactic leading factors recurses into the functor
471	structure and likewise unfolds `pullbackComp` into HEq goals. The promising next route
472	is to reformulate via the adjunction transpose (`(pullbackPushforwardAdjunction _).homEquiv`)
473	so the pentagon becomes a `pullback`-side identity provable by `pseudofunctor_associativity`
474	without touching the strict-pushforward defeq wall, OR to add a strictness-aware
475	`@[simp]` normal form for `pushforward (a≫b)` so the lead peel is syntactic. The bricks
476	`rawPushPullMap`, `pushPullMap_eq_raw`, `pushPull_unit_comp`, `pushforwardComp_hom_app_id`
477	below are the axiom-clean scaffolding for that final step. -/
478	
479	/-- **Relative {\v C}ech complex from a cosimplicial nerve** (coherence-free
480	plumbing). Given `f : X ⟶ S` and an augmented cosimplicial object `N` of
481	`O_X`-modules, produce the relative {\v C}ech cochain complex in `QCoh(S)` by:
482	forgetting the augmentation (`CosimplicialObject.Augmented.drop`), pushing the
483	cosimplicial object forward along `f` (`CosimplicialObject.whiskering` applied to
484	`Scheme.Modules.pushforward f`), and taking the alternating coface-map cochain
485	complex (`alternatingCofaceMapComplex`). This is the entire passage `CechNerve ↦
486	CechComplex`, and it uses no `pushforwardComp` / `pullbackComp` coherence — only
487	the (pre)additivity of `S.Modules`. Project-local. -/
488	noncomputable def relativeCechComplexOfNerve (f : X ⟶ S)
489	    (N : CosimplicialObject.Augmented X.Modules) : CochainComplex S.Modules ℕ :=
490	  (AlgebraicTopology.alternatingCofaceMapComplex S.Modules).obj
491	    (((CosimplicialObject.whiskering X.Modules S.Modules).obj
492	        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward f)).obj (CosimplicialObject.Augmented.drop.obj N))
493	
494	/-- **Relative Čech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf** (Stacks, Cohomology of
495	Schemes, `lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial`).
496	
497	For `f : X ⟶ S`, a finite affine open cover `𝒰` of `X` (with all intersections
498	affine, e.g. `X` separated) and a quasi-coherent sheaf `F`, the *relative Čech
499	complex* `Č•(𝒰, F)` is the cochain complex of `O_S`-modules with degree-`p` term
500	```
501	  Čᵖ(𝒰, F) = ∏_{(i₀,…,i_p)} (f|_{U_{i₀…i_p}})_* (F|_{U_{i₀…i_p}}),
502	```
503	and differential the alternating sum of the restriction maps
504	`(d s)_{i₀…i_{p+1}} = Σⱼ (-1)ʲ s_{i₀…î_j…i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i₀…i_{p+1}}}`. Over an
505	affine `U = Spec A` with `F|_U = M~` and a standard cover by the `D(fᵢ)`, this is
506	the complex of localisations `∏ M_{f_{i₀}} → ∏ M_{f_{i₀}f_{i₁}} → ⋯`. Each term
507	is quasi-coherent because the intersections are affine and the pushforward of a
508	quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated morphism is
509	quasi-coherent.
510	
511	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
512	`lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial`. -/
513	noncomputable def CechComplex (f : X ⟶ S) (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) (F : X.Modules) :
514	    CochainComplex S.Modules ℕ :=
515	  -- Construction (Stacks): apply the relative pushforward `f_*` over each finite
516	  -- intersection to the Čech nerve `CechNerve 𝒰 F`, then take the alternating-sum
517	  -- Čech differential. This is exactly the coherence-free plumbing
518	  -- `relativeCechComplexOfNerve`, so `CechComplex` is genuinely *defined* in terms
519	  -- of the nerve: an axiom-clean `CechNerve` immediately yields an axiom-clean
520	  -- `CechComplex`. The only remaining hole is `CechNerve` itself.
521	  relativeCechComplexOfNerve f (CechNerve 𝒰 F)
522	
523	/-- **Čech acyclicity on affines** (Stacks 02KG;
524	`lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial` and
525	`lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero`).
526	
527	Let `X = Spec A` be affine, `F` a quasi-coherent `O_X`-module, and `𝒰` a finite
528	standard open cover (the `fᵢ ∈ A` generate the unit ideal). Then the relative
529	Čech complex (here with `f` an affine morphism) has vanishing cohomology in all
530	positive degrees: `Hᵖ = 0` for `p ≥ 1`, equivalently `Hᵖ(X, F) = 0` for `p > 0`
531	(Serre vanishing for quasi-coherent sheaves on affines).
532	
533	The proof (Stacks): write `F|_X = M~`; the Čech complex of the standard cover is
534	the complex of localisations, and `Hᵖ = 0` for `p > 0` is equivalent to exactness
535	of the extended complex `0 → M → ∏ M_{f_{i₀}} → ⋯`. Exactness is checked after
536	localising at an arbitrary prime `𝔭`; choosing `i_fix` with `f_{i_fix} ∉ 𝔭`, the
537	prescription `h(s)_{i₀…i_p} = s_{i_fix i₀…i_p}` is a contracting homotopy, so the
538	localised complex is exact, hence so is the complex. The Čech-to-cohomology
539	comparison on the basis of affine opens then gives the sheaf statement. -/
540	theorem CechAcyclic.affine [IsAffine X] (f : X ⟶ S) [IsAffineHom f]
541	    (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) [Finite 𝒰.I₀]
542	    (F : X.Modules) (hF : F.IsQuasicoherent) (p : ℕ) (hp : 1 ≤ p) :
543	    IsZero ((CechComplex f 𝒰 F).homology p) := by
544	  -- Proof (Stacks 02KG): on the affine `X = Spec A` the Čech complex of the
545	  -- standard cover is the complex of localisations; positive-degree exactness
546	  -- follows from the prime-local contracting homotopy `h(s)_{i₀…i_p} =
547	  -- s_{i_fix i₀…i_p}` (where `f_{i_fix} ∉ 𝔭`), giving `(dh + hd) = id`. Needs the
548	  -- explicit localisation description of `CechComplex` on affines and the
549	  -- module-level homotopy, currently absent from Mathlib for `Scheme.Modules`.
550	  sorry

552	/-- **The Čech complex computes the higher direct images** (Stacks 02KE;
553	`lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent` and
554	`lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application`).
555	
556	Let `f : X ⟶ S` be separated and quasi-compact, `F` a quasi-coherent
557	`O_X`-module, and `𝒰` a finite affine open cover of `X` (so, by separatedness,
558	every intersection is affine). Then the cohomology sheaves of the relative Čech
559	complex compute the higher direct images: for every `i ≥ 0` there is a canonical
560	isomorphism of `O_S`-modules
561	```
562	  Hⁱ(Č•(𝒰, F)) ≅ Rⁱ f_* F.
563	```
564	In particular, over an affine base `S = Spec A`, taking global sections gives
565	`Hⁱ(X, F) = Čⁱ(𝒰, F) = H⁰(S, Rⁱ f_* F)` as `A`-modules.
566	
567	We state the isomorphism as `Nonempty (… ≅ …)` and compare against the
568	derived-functor higher direct image `higherDirectImage` wherever the latter is
569	defined (`HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules`).
570	
571	The proof (Stacks 02KE): the question is local on `S`, reducing to `S` affine; by
572	affine acyclicity (`CechAcyclic.affine`) the higher cohomology of `F` over each
573	affine intersection vanishes, so the Čech-to-cohomology spectral sequence
574	collapses to its `q = 0` row, identifying Čech cohomology with sheaf cohomology;
575	over affine `S` the Leray spectral sequence then degenerates (Serre vanishing for
576	the quasi-coherent `Rⁱ f_* F`), giving the stated isomorphism. -/
577	theorem cech_computes_higherDirectImage [HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]
578	    (f : X ⟶ S) [QuasiCompact f] [IsSeparated f] (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) [Finite 𝒰.I₀]
579	    (F : X.Modules) (hF : F.IsQuasicoherent) (i : ℕ) :
580	    Nonempty ((CechComplex f 𝒰 F).homology i ≅ higherDirectImage f i F) := by
581	  -- Proof (Stacks 02KE): reduce to `S` affine; affine acyclicity
582	  -- (`CechAcyclic.affine`) collapses the Čech-to-cohomology spectral sequence to
583	  -- its `q = 0` row, and the Leray spectral sequence degenerates by Serre
584	  -- vanishing for the quasi-coherent `Rⁱ f_* F`, yielding the comparison iso.
585	  -- Needs the two spectral sequences for `Scheme.Modules`, currently absent from
586	  -- Mathlib.
587	  sorry
588	
589	/-- **The unconditional higher direct image via Čech** (Stacks
590	`lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application`; unconditional packaging
591	is Archon-original).
592	
593	For `f : X ⟶ S` separated quasi-compact, `F` quasi-coherent and a finite affine
594	open cover `𝒰` of `X`, the *(unconditional) `i`-th higher direct image* is the
595	`i`-th cohomology sheaf of the relative Čech complex,
596	```
597	  Rⁱ f_* F := Hⁱ(Č•(𝒰, F)) ∈ QCoh(S).
598	```
599	This requires **no** enough-injectives hypothesis: the right-hand side is the
600	cohomology of an explicit complex of quasi-coherent sheaves. By
601	`cech_computes_higherDirectImage` it agrees with the derived-functor higher
602	direct image wherever the latter is defined, and is independent of the chosen
603	affine cover up to canonical isomorphism. For `i = 0` one recovers the ordinary
604	pushforward `R⁰ f_* F = f_* F`. -/
605	noncomputable def cechHigherDirectImage (f : X ⟶ S) (𝒰 : X.OpenCover)
606	    (F : X.Modules) (i : ℕ) : S.Modules :=
607	  (CechComplex f 𝒰 F).homology i
608	
609	/-- **Flat base change for the Čech higher direct images** (Stacks 02KH,
610	`lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology`).
611	
612	Given the cartesian square
613	```
614	  X' --g'--> X
615	  |f'        |f
616	  v          v
617	  S' --g---> S
618	```
619	with `f` separated and quasi-compact, `F` quasi-coherent, `F' = (g')^* F`, and
620	`g` flat, for every `i ≥ 0` the canonical base-change map between the
621	unconditional Čech higher direct images is an isomorphism
622	```
623	  g^*(Rⁱ f_* F) ≅ Rⁱ f'_* ((g')^* F).
624	```
625	Equivalently, for `S = Spec A`, `S' = Spec B` with `A → B` flat, the comparison
626	`Hⁱ(X, F) ⊗_A B → Hⁱ(X', F')` of `B`-modules is an isomorphism.
627	
628	We state the isomorphism as `Nonempty (… ≅ …)`; `𝒰` and `𝒰'` are finite affine
629	covers of `X` and `X' = X ×_S S'` (the latter the base change of the former).
630	
631	The proof (Stacks 02KH): local on `S'`, reduce to `S = Spec A`, `S' = Spec B`,
632	`A → B` flat. Base changing the cover, the affine base change for the `i = 0`
633	direct image identifies each term of the base-changed Čech complex with the
634	original tensored over `A` with `B`, giving `Č•(𝒰_B, F_B) ≅ Č•(𝒰, F) ⊗_A B`;
635	flatness of `A → B` makes `- ⊗_A B` exact, so it commutes with `Hⁱ`, yielding the
636	isomorphism. -/
637	theorem cech_flatBaseChange
638	    (f : X ⟶ S) (g : S' ⟶ S) (f' : X' ⟶ S') (g' : X' ⟶ X)
639	    (h : IsPullback g' f' f g) [Flat g] [QuasiCompact f] [IsSeparated f]
640	    (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) [Finite 𝒰.I₀] (𝒰' : X'.OpenCover) [Finite 𝒰'.I₀]
641	    (F : X.Modules) (hF : F.IsQuasicoherent) (i : ℕ) :
642	    Nonempty ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g).obj (cechHigherDirectImage f 𝒰 F i) ≅
643	      cechHigherDirectImage f' 𝒰' ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g').obj F) i) := by
644	  -- Proof (Stacks 02KH): local on `S'`, reduce to `S = Spec A`, `S' = Spec B`,
645	  -- `A → B` flat; base change of the cover and the affine `i = 0` base change give
646	  -- `Č•(𝒰_B, F_B) ≅ Č•(𝒰, F) ⊗_A B`, and flatness makes `- ⊗_A B` commute with
647	  -- `Hⁱ`. Needs the term-wise affine base change of the Čech complex and exactness
648	  -- of `- ⊗_A B` on `Scheme.Modules`, currently absent from Mathlib.
649	  sorry
650	
651	end AlgebraicGeometry

8	/-!
9	# Flattening stratification of a coherent sheaf (A.2.a)
10	
11	This file is the **A.2.a** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
12	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the
13	Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem — the technical heart that
14	turns the absolute Quot-functor representability problem on a projective
15	morphism `π : X ⟶ S` with coherent input `𝓕` into a finite collection of
16	locally-closed strata over which `𝓕` becomes flat.
17	
18	## Status (iter-176 Lane E file-skeleton — re-dispatch)
19	
20	iter-175 Lane E died to the Anthropic session-limit reset window without
21	ever calling `Write` (the file was never created). iter-176 re-dispatches
22	the file-skeleton verbatim. Each blueprint-pinned declaration carries the
23	*intended* substantive type signature (matching the `\lean{...}` pin in
24	`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_FlatteningStratification.tex`) with a
25	`sorry` body. The bodies are iter-177+ work; the substantive proofs are
26	deep (Nitsure~\S4 ~250-line argument: generic-flatness ⟶ Noetherian
27	induction ⟶ Castelnuovo–Mumford regularity ⟶ assembly via direct images
28	`E_i = π_* 𝓕(N+i)`).
29	
30	The 4 blueprint-pinned declarations are:
31	
32	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat` (def, ~8 LOC) — the
33	   **coherent-sheaf flatness predicate** for a coherent `𝓕` on `X` over
34	   `S` via `f : X ⟶ S`. Affine-locally: for `U ⊆ S` and `V ⊆ f⁻¹U` both
35	   affine, `Γ(𝓕, V)` is a flat `Γ(S, U)`-module via the composite
36	   `Γ(S, U) → Γ(X, V) → End(Γ(𝓕, V))`. The corresponding Stacks tag is
37	   00HB (module-level) lifted to a sheaf-level predicate by the
38	   affine-local descent characterisation.
39	
40	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness` (theorem, ~15 LOC) — **generic
41	   flatness** [Nitsure §4 Theorem]: over a noetherian integral base
42	   `S`, a coherent sheaf on a finite-type `X ⟶ S` is flat over a
43	   non-empty open `V ⊆ S`.
44	
45	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification` (theorem, ~25 LOC) — the
46	   **flattening-stratification existence theorem** [Nitsure §4]: for `S`
47	   noetherian and `𝓕` coherent on a proper `π : X ⟶ S`, there exists a
48	   finite locally-closed stratification `{S_f}` of `S` (set-theoretically
49	   covering, pairwise disjoint) such that `𝓕` becomes flat on each
50	   stratum.
51	
52	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification_universal` (theorem, ~15 LOC)
53	   — the **universal property** of the stratification [Nitsure §4 (ii)]:
54	   any morphism `φ : T ⟶ S` such that `𝓕` pulled back to `X ×_S T` is
55	   `T`-flat factors uniquely through the stratum coproduct
56	   `i : ⨿ S_f ⟶ S`.
57	
58	Additionally, three sub-lemmas decompose the existence proof, plus a
59	curve-specialisation corollary feeding the Route~A consumer:
60	
61	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatLocusStratification` (lemma, ~12 LOC) — the
62	   **special case `n = 0`** [Nitsure §4, n=0]: a coherent `𝓕` on `S`
63	   admits a finite locally-closed stratification by integer "rank"
64	   strata.
65	
66	6. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatLocusReduction` (lemma, ~12 LOC) — the
67	   **Noetherian-induction reduction** [Nitsure §4 general case opening]:
68	   a finite reduced locally-closed stratification on which `𝓕`
69	   *already* becomes flat (without polynomial indexing).
70	
71	7. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatLocusAssembly` (lemma, ~15 LOC) — the
72	   **assembly step** [Nitsure §4 via direct images]: combining the
73	   `n = 0` stratification iteratively on the direct images
74	   `E_i = π_* 𝓕(N+i)` to refine to the Hilbert-polynomial-indexed
75	   stratification.
76	
77	8. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification.ofCurve` (theorem, ~10 LOC)
78	   — the **relative-curve specialisation** for the Route~A consumer
79	   `C ×_k T → T`.
80	
81	## Note on type expressivity
82	
83	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof",
84	each declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
85	
86	- `CoherentSheafFlat` reduces to module-theoretic flatness of every
87	  affine-local section module over the corresponding affine-base ring
88	  via the `appLE` ring-hom — non-empty content because flatness of a
89	  module is the standard non-trivial homological condition.
90	- `genericFlatness` requires the existence of a *non-empty* open `V`
91	  and substantive flatness on every affine `U ⊆ V`; both quantifiers
92	  are necessary (without them the statement collapses to `V = ∅`).
93	- `flatteningStratification` packages a *finite* indexed family of
94	  *locally-closed-immersions* `ι : S_f ⟶ S` that pairwise *cover*
95	  `|S|` *disjointly* AND on which the pullback `𝓕|_{X ×_S S_f}` becomes
96	  `S_f`-flat — all four conjuncts are substantive (any single
97	  one trivialises if dropped).
98	- `flatteningStratification_universal` packages the universal property
99	  via `∃!` (unique existence of factorisation), a substantive Yoneda
100	  bijection content.
101	
102	## Mathlib status
103	
104	Mathlib (master `b80f227`) provides `Module.Flat`,
105	`AlgebraicGeometry.Flat` (morphism-level), `IsImmersion`,
106	`IsLocallyNoetherian`, `IsIntegral`, `LocallyOfFiniteType`. It does NOT
107	provide: a relative projective space `ℙⁿ_S`, an `IsProjective` morphism
108	property, the flattening-stratification construction, or coherent-sheaf
109	flatness as a sheaf-level predicate.
110	
111	The current file-skeleton uses `IsProper π` as the structural stand-in
112	for "projective `π`" (every projective morphism is proper; the
113	restriction is harmless in the Route~A consumer setting where `π` comes
114	from a smooth proper curve, which is automatically projective).
115	iter-177+ refinement: once Mathlib gains an `IsProjective` morphism
116	property, the hypothesis tightens.
117	
118	## References
119	
120	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_FlatteningStratification.tex`
121	(735 LOC, 4 pins + 4 sub-lemma/corollary names). Source: Nitsure,
122	"Construction of Hilbert and Quot schemes", §4 (FGA Explained Ch. 5,
123	arXiv:math/0504020 pp. 5–18); Stacks Project tags 00HB (module flat),
124	052H (flattening stratification).
125	-/
